title: Divorce Process in Bangladesh: Complete Legal Guide (2026)
metaTitle: Divorce Process in Bangladesh – Complete Legal Guide 2026
metaDescription: How to file for divorce in Bangladesh — talaq, khula, mutual divorce, Family Court process, required documents, costs and timelines. Expert guide by a divorce lawyer in Dhaka.
heroIntro: Divorce is one of the most emotionally and legally complex situations a person can face. In Bangladesh, the process differs depending on who initiates it and the religion of the parties. Understanding your options clearly — before you take any action — can protect your rights and avoid costly mistakes.
toc: Types of Divorce Under Bangladesh Law
How Long Does Divorce Take in Bangladesh?

sections.h2: Types of Divorce Under Bangladesh Law
sections.content:
In Bangladesh, Muslim personal law governs divorce for the Muslim majority, under the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 (MFLO). Divorce for non-Muslims is governed by their respective personal laws (Christian Divorce Act 1869, Hindu personal law etc.). This guide focuses primarily on Muslim divorce law, which applies to the majority of Bangladeshi citizens.

Muslim divorce in Bangladesh takes three main forms: Talaq (husband-initiated), Khula (wife-initiated), and Mubarat (mutual consent). Each has a different process, legal consequences, and timeline. A divorce lawyer in Dhaka or Uttara can advise which route is best for your situation.

Talaq is the husband's right to pronounce divorce under Islamic law. Under the MFLO 1961, the process in Bangladesh is:

  1. The husband pronounces talaq (in writing, in person, or verbally).
  2. He must immediately send a written notice to the Chairman of the local Union Parishad (or City Corporation Ward Commissioner) and simultaneously provide a copy to the wife.
  3. The Chairman constitutes an Arbitration Council to attempt reconciliation.
  4. A mandatory 90-day waiting period begins from the date the Chairman receives notice.
  5. If reconciliation fails within 90 days, the divorce becomes effective on day 90.

Important: A talaq pronounced without the required notice to the Chairman is an offence under the MFLO and the divorce does not become legally effective until notice is properly given. Many people skip this step and create serious legal complications.

Khula is the wife's right to seek divorce from her husband, typically by returning part or all of her mahr (dower). This is a negotiated separation. The process:

  1. The wife initiates negotiations with her husband for a khula.
  2. Both parties agree on what consideration (if any) the wife will return to obtain her freedom.
  3. The agreed terms are documented and the divorce notice must similarly be sent to the Union Parishad Chairman.
  4. The same 90-day reconciliation period applies.

If the husband refuses khula, the wife's alternative is to file a divorce suit in Family Court seeking a judicial divorce on specific legal grounds. A family lawyer specialising in women's rights can assist in this situation.

A Mubarat (mutual consent divorce) is the cleanest and fastest route when both spouses agree to end the marriage. Both parties jointly send the separation notice to the Union Parishad Chairman. The 90-day period still applies, but since both agree, reconciliation efforts typically conclude early.

Mutual divorces allow both parties to agree on financial terms, child custody, and property division in advance — reducing future litigation. It is strongly advisable to have a lawyer document these agreed terms formally.

A wife can file for divorce through the Family Court when her husband refuses to grant a khula or if specific grounds exist. Legal grounds for judicial divorce in Bangladesh include:

  • Failure to pay maintenance for two years
  • Husband sentenced to imprisonment for seven or more years
  • Husband's failure to perform marital obligations for three years
  • Husband's insanity (for two or more years)
  • Cruelty (physical or mental)
  • Husband's impotence at the time of marriage
  • Husband taking a second wife without proper permission (MFLO 1961)

File a plaint at the Family Court in the district where you or your husband resides. For cases in Dhaka Metropolitan area including Uttara, the relevant Family Court is in Dhaka. See: Divorce lawyer Uttara Dhaka.

One of the most misunderstood aspects of Bangladeshi divorce law is the mandatory 90-day period. During this period:

  • The Union Parishad Chairman attempts to reconcile the couple
  • The divorce does not take legal effect until the 90 days expire without reconciliation
  • If the wife is pregnant, the iddat period (after which the divorce takes effect) may be extended to the delivery of the child
  • The husband cannot remarry during the 90 days (the wife is still his legal spouse)

After successful completion of the 90-day process, obtain a divorce certificate from the Union Parishad — this is the official document you need for all future legal purposes.

• Original Kabinnama (marriage registration certificate)
  • National Identity Cards of both parties
  • Written talaq notice (for husband-initiated divorce)
  • Proof of sending notice to Union Parishad Chairman
  • Any prior court orders or mediation agreements
  • For Family Court suits: plaint, supporting affidavit, proof of grounds

Divorce does not extinguish financial obligations. After divorce in Bangladesh:

  • Mahr (dower): The full outstanding mahr becomes immediately payable to the divorced wife.
  • Iddat maintenance: The husband must maintain the wife for the iddat period (~90 days) after divorce is effective.
  • Child maintenance: The father remains obligated to maintain all children regardless of who has custody.
  • Stridhan (dowry items): All jewellery and gifts given to the wife at marriage must be returned.

These rights are fully enforceable through the Family Court. Do not accept verbal assurances — get everything in writing and have a lawyer verify the agreement.

sections.h2: How Long Does Divorce Take in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
Timeline depends on the type of divorce:

  • Mutual (Mubarat): Minimum 90 days from the date the Chairman receives notice, assuming no reconciliation is achieved.
  • Talaq by husband: Also a minimum of 90 days from written notice to Chairman.
  • Judicial divorce (Family Court): Six months to several years depending on court workload and whether the matter is contested.

The 90-day minimum is fixed by law and cannot be shortened. For contested cases, engaging an experienced family lawyer in Dhaka significantly reduces delays and protects your rights throughout the process.

faqs.question: Can a wife refuse divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: A wife cannot prevent a husband from pronouncing talaq, but she can ensure the legal process is followed correctly. If she has financial or custody concerns, the Family Court provides remedies.

faqs.question: What is the difference between talaq and khula in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Talaq is the husband's right to divorce. Khula is the wife's right to seek divorce (usually by returning mahr). Both require sending notice to the Union Parishad Chairman and waiting 90 days.

faqs.question: Can I remarry immediately after divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Not immediately. After the divorce takes effect (day 90), the wife must complete her iddat period (approximately 3 months) before remarrying. The husband may remarry after the divorce is effective.

faqs.question: Is a divorce valid without a divorce certificate?
faqs.answer: The divorce notice sent to the Chairman and the 90-day period create the legal divorce. However, the Chairman's divorce certificate is the practical document needed for future legal purposes. Always obtain it.

faqs.question: What if my husband is abroad and refuses to give divorce?
faqs.answer: If your Kabinnama includes a delegated right of divorce (talaq-e-tawfiz), you can use it. Otherwise, file for judicial divorce in Family Court even if the husband is abroad.

faqs.question: Does divorce affect children's citizenship or inheritance rights?
faqs.answer: No. Children's citizenship and inheritance rights from both parents are not affected by the parents' divorce.

faqs.question: Can a non-Muslim get divorced in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes, but under different laws. Christians use the Christian Divorce Act 1869 (requires specific grounds and court proceedings). Hindus have limited formal divorce options under personal law in Bangladesh.
